data.title: Chip stocks gain after Fed Chair Powell's speech at Jackson Hole
data.description:
Chip stocks were in the green on Friday after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell signaled the Fed will potentially cut rates in September if data warrants.

Powell delivered the key speech at a central banking symposium in Jackson Hole, Wyoming. It is likely Powell’s last time addressing the marquee event, with his term as Fed chair ending in May.

AI chipmakers Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA) and Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) rose around 2% each on Friday. Broadcom (AVGO) and Qualcomm (QCOM) jumped roughly 3% each.

Shares of NXP Semiconductors (NXPI), Lattice Semiconductor (LSCC), GlobalFoundries (GFS), and STMicroelectronics (STM) surged about 5% each.  Texas Instruments (TXN) and Intel (INTC) jumped nearly 4% each, while Micron Technology (MU) and Marvell Technology (MRVL) each climbed about 3%. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) rose nearly 2%.

Meanwhile, chip equipment makers: Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research (LRCX) and Dutch company ASML (ASML) each climbed nearly 3%, while KLA (KLAC) rose about 2%.

data.title: Shifting risks may warrant rate adjustment, Fed Chair Powell says at Jackson Hole
data.description:
A shifting balance of risks "may warrant adjusting our policy stance," Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said on Friday, indicating that he's open to lowering the central bank's policy rate if the data warrant.

"Risks to inflation are tilted to the upside, and risks to employment to the downside — a challenging situation," he said in his widely anticipated address at the Kansas City's Fed Central Bank Symposium at Jackson Hole, Wyoming. This year, the gathering's theme is "Labor Markets in Transition: Demographics, Productivity and Macroeconomic Policy."

With the U.S. inflation level remaining persistent since 2021, the Federal Reserve reversed its position on inflation-averaging that it had adopted in its 2020 framework review and eliminated the "makeup" strategy in its 2025 revision. "As it turned out, the idea of an intentional, moderate inflation overshoot had proved irrelevant," Powell said.

The 2020 statement also said the Fed would mitigate "shortfalls," rather than "deviations" from maximum employment. With this framework revision, the central bank is removing the word "shortfalls" as the term "wasn't always interpreted as intended."

"Instead, the revised document now states more precisely that 'the Committee recognizes that employment may at times run above real-time assessments of maximum employment without necessarily creating risks to price stability,'" he said.

Regarding the economic and monetary policy outlook, Powell said the much larger than usual downward revision to May and June's jobs numbers does not appear to have "opened up a large margin of slack in the labor market — an outcome we want to avoid."

Unemployment remaining at a still-low level of 4.2% and some other labor market indicators are little changed, he added.  And while the labor market demand has softened some, so has the market's supply. "Overall, while the labor market appears to be in balance, it is a curious kind of balance that results from a marked slowing in both the supply of and demand for workers,"  he said. "This unusual situation suggests that downside risks to employment are rising."

If those risks materialize, they may do so quickly through rising layoffs and climbing unemployment.

He also repeated the potential scenarios for how tariffs may affect inflation — either a short-lived, one-time price increase or persistent inflationary pressures. "The effects of tariffs on consumer prices are now clearly visible," Powell said. "We expect those effects to accumulate over coming months, with high uncertainty about timing and amounts."

The possibility of a relatively short-lived shift in price levels from tariffs is "a reasonable base case," he said.

"It is also possible, however, that the upward pressure on prices from tariffs could spur a more lasting inflation dynamic, and that is a risk to be assessed and managed."

Another potential outcome is that inflation expectations can climb, "dragging actual inflation with them."

"Come what may, we will not allow a one-time increase in the price level to become an ongoing inflation problem," the Fed chair said. As usual, he vowed that the Federal Open Market Committee will base its decisions "solely on their assessment of the data and its implications for the economic outlook and the balance of risks. We will never deviate from that approach."

data.title: Fed rate cut odds rise after Powell's Jackson Hole speech
data.description:
The odds of a rate cut in September rose after Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech at Jackson Hole. 

The chance of a September rate cut was at ~71% before the speech and rose to ~93% after it. 

Chances of a September rate cut had plunged below 50% in July after Powell's hawkish remarks and had fallen below 40% after a stubborn inflation report. But the August jobs data has caused a significant reversal, sending the odds back above 80%.

Wall Street also gained ground on Friday as investors reacted positively to Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech at Jackson Hole.

The rise in rate cut odds comes at a time when U.S. President Donald Trump has repeatedly criticized the Fed and Chair Powell for not lowering interest rates.

After Powell's speech at the Jackson Hole Symposium, it is now widely expected that the Fed will slash its rates by a quarter point.

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS), and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: Wall Street rallies after Powell speaks at Jackson Hole
data.description:
Wall Street has gained ground on Friday as investors react positively to Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell's speech at Jackson Hole.

The tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) has risen by  1.7%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) has moved up by 1.5%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) has pushed up by 1.7%.

Across sectors, all of the 11 S&P groups are trading higher, with Consumer Discretionary and Real Estate leading the advance. Consumer Staples, meanwhile, is the session’s weakest performer. 

Over in the bond market yields are shifting lower as investors buy bonds. The longer-end U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) has declined by 5 basis points to 4.27%, and the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) has fell 9 basis points to 3.69%.

"Powell's dovish Jackson Hole comments suggest the Federal Reserve is ready to cut interest rates in September, which is just what investors were hoping to hear, given the recent slowdown in the labor market.  While there is still one more employment report before the September meeting, it's clear the Fed has enough data under its belt to justify a September cut," Scott Gamm of Strategy Voice Associates stated.

Aside from the headline of Powells speech at Jackson Hole, the economic calendar is light otherwise.

As for stocks that are on the move, shares of Intuit (INTU) slipped 6.1% after the financial software firm reported its latest quarterly earnings.

data.title: Treasury's Michael Faulkender steps down after five months - report
data.description:
Deputy Treasury Secretary Michael Faulkender is stepping down less than five months after his Senate confirmation, according to a media report published Friday.

This marks the second top Treasury departure this month, after U.S. President Donald Trump is ousted Internal Revenue Service Commisioner Billy Long just two months into the job.

Faulkender worked in the Treasury Department during Trump's first term. In the current term, Faulkender headed Treasury's transition team and helped shape the tax policies in Trump's tax and spending package signed last month.

Treasury did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

data.title: Housing market still stretched even with potential rate cuts – BofA analysts
data.description:
Housing affordability remains stretched even with potential rate cuts, requiring better wage growth or increased supply for meaningful improvement, according to Rafe Jadrosich, Senior U.S. Homebuilders and Building Products analyst.

The prospect of rate cuts has boosted homebuilding stocks (NYSEARCA:XHB), which have risen 16% since the end of June, outperforming the broader market in a pattern consistent with previous rate-cutting cycles.

“This recent rally reminds us a lot of what you saw in July and August of 2024 ahead of the September rate cut,” said Jadrosich, during a podcast conducted by BofA Global Research.

However, Jadrosich cautioned that “builders are trading now at 1.5-1.6x price-to-book – a company's stock price vs. its book value, which evaluates its market valuation relative to its assets – which is a little bit elevated compared to historical multiples heading into the first Fed rate cut.”

Also, despite 30-year fixed mortgage rates declining from 6.9% to 6.6% earlier this year, Jadrosich indicated in the interview that this 30-basis-point move has not significantly impacted demand.

“The new home market will certainly benefit if you have lower rates, especially if that’s in a backdrop where the macro environment is stable, it does increase demand, but affordability is still really stretched at current levels,” he said.

Jadrosich emphasized that “affordability probably only gets solved with better wage growth or more supply that comes onto the market.”

Housing markets are showing pronounced regional differences, with the Northeast, Mid-Atlantic, and Midwest outperforming while Florida and Texas experience weakness.

“Within Florida, it’s the entry level markets that are the softest, Jacksonville would stand out, and then some of the markets in southwest Florida that have been impacted by the cost of insurance,” Jadrosich explained.

He added that in Texas, “some of the Covid winners, cities like Austin and San Antonio that saw a lot of home price appreciation and in-migration, that’s where you’re seeing price cuts today.”

On a positive note, there appears to be encouraging progress in federal housing policy, with the Senate Banking Committee recently passing the ROAD to Housing Act of 2025.

According to Jadrosich, this bipartisan bill is “designed to spur construction and more housing through a mix of regulatory changes, studies, pilots, grant programs.”

While he cautioned that housing remains primarily a local issue, Jadrosich noted that the bill could “empower the Federal government through HUD to play a more useful role in tackling the housing shortage.”

Mark Cabana, head of U.S. Rates Strategy at BofA, also participated in the interview. He questioned if – with some signs of a moderating labor market – monetary policy is truly restrictive given the 4.2% unemployment rate and “very easy financial conditions, equities (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) near all-time highs, credit spreads very tight.”

With the market pricing in a 75.5% probability of an interest rate cut of about 25 basis points at the September FOMC meeting, he believes a dovish Fed could push long rates lower despite inflation concerns.

data.title: UK regulator begins probe into Shutterstock/Getty Images deal
data.description:
The UK's antitrust regulator started an investigation into Getty Images's (NYSE:GETY) planned acquisition of Shutterstock (NYSE:SSTK). 

The UK's Competition and Markets Authority has a deadline of Oct. 20 to make a decision on the transaction, according to a filing on the regulator's website.

Getty Images (NYSE:GETY) agreed to purchase Shutterstock (SSTK) in a cash and stock deal in January, creating a new company valued at about $3.7 billion, including debt.

Shares of Getty Images (GETY) rose 5.8% on Friday, while Shutterstock gained 3%.

data.title: Tempus AI will acquire Paige for $81.25M
data.description:
• Tempus AI (NASDAQ:TEM) said that it will acquire Paige, an AI company focused on digital pathology, for $81.25M.
  • The deal amount is being paid predominantly in Tempus stock, as well as its assumption of Paige’s commitment under its existing Microsoft Azure cloud services deal.
  • The stock is up about 2% so far today.

data.title: U.S. stablecoin law spurs EU to accelerate digital euro plans - report
data.description:
Policymakers in the European Union are fast-tracking work on a digital euro after the U.S.'s landmark stablecoin law deepened concerns that dollar-backed tokens could crowd out Europe's currency in digital markets, according to a recent media report.

Last month, U.S. Congress passed the GENIUS Act, which set a new regulatory framework for the stablecoin sector and its issuers. A stablecoin is a digital token designed to keep a steady value to assets like the dollar.

After U.S. President Donald Trump signed the stablecoin legislation into law, EU policymakers started revisiting their digital euro plans, the Financial Times reported.

EU officials are weighing a shift from the once-expected private network to a public blockchain like Ethereum (ETH-USD) or Solana (SOL-USD), people familiar with the matter told the FT.

It's been years in which the European Central Bank has studied a digital euro. The monetary authority was said in May it was teaming up with nearly EU market participants to test the digital euro's payment functionalities and use cases through a newly launched innovation hub.

Central bank digital currencies, like the digital euro, are digital versions of cash issued and guaranteed by a country's central bank.

data.title: Honeywell names FARO Technologies CEO Lau to lead industrial automation unit
data.description:
Honeywell (NASDAQ:HON) said Friday it appointed former FARO Technologies head Peter Lau as the new President and CEO of its Industrial Automation business, effective October 15.

Lau, a former President of Honeywell's (NASDAQ:HON) Security, Fire and Electrical Products businesses, is returning to the company to succeed Lucian Boldea, who is leaving at the end of the month to pursue another opportunity.

Lau most recently served as President and CEO of 3D measuring systems company FARO Technologies, which was acquired in May by industrial tools maker Ametek, and previously served in leadership positions at Hubbell, GE and Catalyst Nutraceuticals.

Honeywell recently said it would evaluate strategic alternatives for parts of its Industrial Automation business ahead of the company's planned separation into three independent companies, which is expected to be complete in H2 2026.
